docs.title: BDD::Behave
docs.text:
The latest version of this documentation lives at https://gdonald.github.io/BDD-Behave/.

The homepage for BDD::Behave is https://github.com/gdonald/BDD-Behave.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
BDD::Behave is a behavior-driven development framework for Raku. Specs are ordinary Raku files that use BDD::Behave; and call exported DSL helpers (describe, context, it, let, before-each, expect, …) to build and run a tree of examples.

Currently developed against Raku v6.d.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
specs/001-spec.raku

use BDD::Behave;

describe 'this spec', {
  let(:answer, { 42 });

  it 'passes', {
    expect(:answer).to.be(42);
  }
}

describe 'this final spec', {
  let(:answer, { 42 });

  it 'fails at line 12', {
    expect(:answer).to.be(41);
  }
}

Run it with the behave runner:

$ behave specs/001-spec.raku

Output:

specs/001-spec.raku

⮑  'this spec'
  ⮑  'passes'
      ⮑  SUCCESS

⮑  'this final spec'
  ⮑  'fails at line 12'
      ⮑  FAILURE

Failures:

  [ ✗ ] specs/001-spec.raku:12

2 examples, 1 failed, 1 passed

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
BDD::Behave can be installed using the zef module installation tool:

zef install BDD::Behave

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
A Behave project keeps spec files in a specs/ directory at the project root. The behave runner picks up any file matching spec.raku (e.g. 001-basic-spec.raku, users-spec.raku, subdir/admin-spec.raku).

my-project/
├── lib/
│   └── MyApp.rakumod
└── specs/
    ├── basic-spec.raku
    └── users/
        └── auth-spec.raku

docs.title: Your first spec
docs.text:
Every spec file starts with use BDD::Behave; and then declares one or more top-level describe blocks.

use BDD::Behave;

describe 'arithmetic', {
  it 'adds integers', {
    expect(1 + 1).to.be(2);
  }

  it 'multiplies integers', {
    expect(3 * 4).to.be(12);
  }
}

docs.title: Running specs
docs.text:
Run all specs found in specs/:

behave

Run a single spec file:

behave specs/basic-spec.raku

During local development of an app whose lib/ is not yet installed, prefix with raku -Ilib:

raku -Ilib bin/behave specs/basic-spec.raku

See Running Specs for the full set of options.

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• describe / context — group related examples
  • it — define an example
  • let — lazy, memoized values per example
  • Hooks — before-each, after-each, before-all, after-all
  • expect — assertions

docs.title: Running Specs
docs.text: The behave command runs Behave specs.

docs.title: Default behavior
docs.text:
With no arguments, behave looks for a specs/ directory under the current working directory and runs every file matching spec.raku (recursively).

$ behave

docs.title: Selecting files
docs.text:
Pass one or more spec file paths to run a subset:

$ behave specs/users-spec.raku specs/admin-spec.raku

docs.title: Local development
docs.text:
When you're working on Behave itself (or your project's lib/ is not yet installed), tell Raku where to find the modules:

$ raku -Ilib bin/behave
$ raku -Ilib bin/behave specs/some-spec.raku

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Effect --help Display usage --verbose Print each spec file as it is loaded

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Behave prints each describe/context with a ⮑ marker, indenting nested groups, and reports SUCCESS / FAILURE / PENDING per example. After all suites run it prints a summary like:

============================================================
Overall: 96 examples
  96 passed

docs.title: Exit code
docs.text: behave exits 0 if every example passed, 1 if any example failed.

docs.title: Tests
docs.text:
Behave has two test surfaces:

  1. Specs in specs/ — Behave's own behavioral test suite, run with the behave runner.
  2. Unit tests in t/ — Test-module tests that exercise individual classes (Runner, SpecTree, DSL helpers, etc.), run with prove6.

docs.title: Running the specs
docs.text:
$ raku -Ilib bin/behave

This loads every *spec.raku file under specs/ and runs them through the Behave runner.

docs.title: Running the unit tests
docs.text:
$ prove6 -Ilib t

You can also run a single test file directly:

$ raku -Ilib t/dsl/hooks.rakutest

docs.title: Both at once
docs.text:
When developing Behave itself, run both before considering a change done:

$ prove6 -Ilib t && raku -Ilib bin/behave

docs.title: Continuous integration
docs.text: Pushes and pull requests run the full suite via the GitHub Actions workflow. The same workflow builds and publishes this documentation site to GitHub Pages on every push to main.

docs.title: describe / context
docs.text: describe and context define a group of related examples. They are aliases — pick whichever reads better in plain English. Groups can be nested arbitrarily.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
use BDD::Behave;

describe 'String methods', {
  it 'uppercases', {
    expect('hi'.uc).to.be('HI');
  }

  it 'reverses', {
    expect('abc'.flip).to.be('cba');
  }
}

docs.title: Nesting with context
docs.text:
context reads naturally for branching scenarios:

describe 'User#full-name', {
  context 'when both names are present', {
    it 'joins them with a space', {
      my $u = User.new(:first<Ada>, :last<Lovelace>);
      expect($u.full-name).to.be('Ada Lovelace');
    }
  }

  context 'when only the first name is present', {
    it 'returns the first name alone', {
      my $u = User.new(:first<Ada>);
      expect($u.full-name).to.be('Ada');
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Scoping
docs.text:
Plain Raku scoping rules apply inside a group block — my $foo = 'bar' declared inside a describe is visible to its examples and nested groups, and shadowing works as you'd expect.

describe 'shadowing', {
  my $value = 'outer';

  it 'sees the outer value', {
    expect($value).to.be('outer');
  }

  context 'inner', {
    my $value = 'inner';

    it 'sees the inner value', {
      expect($value).to.be('inner');
    }
  }
}

docs.title: expect
docs.text: expect builds an expectation about an actual value. The current matcher is be, which uses Raku's smartmatch operator (~~).

docs.title: Basic form
docs.text:
expect(actual).to.be(expected);

expect returns a builder, .to is a no-op for readability, and .be(expected) performs the comparison.

it 'compares values', {
  expect(1 + 1).to.be(2);
  expect('hi'.uc).to.be('HI');
  expect(@list).to.be([1, 2, 3]);
}

docs.title: Smartmatch semantics
docs.text:
Because be uses ~~, you can match against types, regexes, ranges, junctions, and anything else Raku smartmatches:

expect(42).to.be(Int);              # type
expect('hello').to.be(/hell/);      # regex
expect(5).to.be(1..10);             # range
expect($x).to.be(any(1, 2, 3));     # junction

docs.title: Negation
docs.text:
.not flips the comparison:

expect(1 + 1).to.not.be(3);

docs.title: Reading from let
docs.text:
expect recognises a single named-pair argument as a let lookup:

let(:answer, { 42 });

it 'matches the let', {
  expect(:answer).to.be(42);
}

You can also pass a Pair as the expected value to read from a let:

let(:answer, { 42 });

it 'compares two lets', {
  expect(:answer).to.be(:answer);
}

For more flexible let access, prefer the binding syntax shown in let.

docs.title: Failure output
docs.text: When an expectation fails, the runner records the file and line of the expect call and prints them in the failure summary so you can jump straight to the offending line.

docs.title: Hooks
docs.text:
Hooks let you run setup and teardown code around examples. Behave provides four hooks:

Hook Fires before-all Once, before the first example in the group before-each Before every example in the group (and inherited by nested groups) after-each After every example in the group (and inherited by nested groups) after-all Once, after the last example in the group

All four are exported from BDD::Behave and must be called inside a describe or context block.

docs.title: Basic example
docs.text:
describe 'a counter', {
  my $count;

  before-each {
    $count = 0;
  }

  it 'starts at zero', {
    expect($count).to.be(0);
  }

  it 'is reset between examples', {
    $count++;
    expect($count).to.be(1);
  }
}

docs.title: Ordering and inheritance
docs.text:
Within a group, hooks run in registration order. When examples live in nested groups, outer before-each hooks fire outer-to-inner, and outer after-each hooks fire inner-to-outer:

describe 'outer', {
  before-each { trace('outer-before') }
  after-each  { trace('outer-after')  }

  context 'inner', {
    before-each { trace('inner-before') }
    after-each  { trace('inner-after')  }

    it 'runs', {
      # trace so far: outer-before, inner-before
      trace('example');
    }
    # after the example: inner-after, outer-after
  }
}

docs.title: before-all / after-all
docs.text:
These fire exactly once per group — useful for expensive shared setup.

describe 'database queries', {
  before-all {
    setup-fixture-data;
  }

  after-all {
    teardown-fixture-data;
  }

  it 'finds a user', { ... }
  it 'finds a page', { ... }
}

Warning

Anything before-all mutates is shared across the group's examples. Prefer before-each for per-example isolation.

docs.title: it
docs.text:
it defines a single example. The first argument is a human-readable description, the second is a block containing the test body.

describe 'Array', {
  it 'reports its element count', {
    expect((1, 2, 3).elems).to.be(3);
  }
}

docs.title: Multiple expectations
docs.text:
An example can contain any number of expect calls. Each failing expectation is recorded; the example fails if at least one expectation fails or the block raises an unhandled exception.

it 'a User is valid after construction', {
  my $u = User.new(:name<Ada>, :age(36));
  expect($u.name).to.be('Ada');
  expect($u.age).to.be(36);
  expect($u.valid).to.be(True);
}

docs.title: Pending examples
docs.text: You can mark an example as pending by setting its pending flag on the returned object (advanced). The simplest path is to comment out the body or skip writing the assertion until you implement the behavior.

docs.title: Optional context parameter
docs.text:
If your example block accepts a positional parameter, Behave passes a context object that lets you reach let values via method-style access:

context 'with a let context parameter', {
  let(:widget, { Widget.new(:bar(88)) });

  it 'reads the widget via .', -> $_ {
    expect(.widget.bar).to.be(88);
  }
}

See let for the various ways to define and consume let values.

docs.title: let
docs.text: let defines a value that is lazy (only computed when first read) and memoized per example (reset between examples). Use it for the test subject and any inputs whose construction you'd otherwise repeat.

docs.title: Defining a let
docs.text:
Two equivalent forms:

let('answer', { 42 });   # string name
let(:answer,  { 42 });   # named-argument form

A let declared in a describe or context is visible to every example in that group and its nested groups.

docs.title: Reading a let
docs.text: There are three ways to read a let value:

docs.title: 1. Named-argument form in expect
docs.text:
describe 'answer', {
  let(:answer, { 42 });

  it 'is 42', {
    expect(:answer).to.be(42);
  }
}

docs.title: 2. Binding syntax — := to a normal variable
docs.text:
This is the most flexible form: bind once, then use the variable like any other.

describe 'widget', {
  it 'has the expected fields', {
    my $w := let(:widget, { Widget.new(:bar(99)) });

    expect($w.bar).to.be(99);
    expect($w.baz).to.be(42);
  }
}

docs.title: 3. Context-parameter form
docs.text:
If the example block takes a positional parameter, the context object exposes lets as methods:

let(:widget, { Widget.new(:bar(88)) });

it 'reads via the context parameter', -> $_ {
  expect(.widget.bar).to.be(88);
}

docs.title: Memoization
docs.text:
Within a single example, calling a let multiple times returns the same value:

let(:counter, { $n++ });   # called once per example

it 'memoizes within an example', {
  expect(:counter).to.be(:counter);   # same value both reads
}

Between examples the cache is reset, so each example sees a fresh value.

docs.title: Nested lets and shadowing
docs.text:
Inner let definitions shadow outer ones with the same name.

describe 'shadowing', {
  let(:value, { 'outer' });

  it 'sees outer', { expect(:value).to.be('outer') }

  context 'inside', {
    let(:value, { 'inner' });

    it 'sees inner', { expect(:value).to.be('inner') }
  }
}
